AI.tldr: Proposes ASSCG, an adaptive gate that decides when to query a slow LLM planner vs. cache or drop guidance, improving autonomous driving performance while reducing latency by 60%.
AI.motivation: LLMs improve driving planning but are costly to query; existing fast-slow planners use rigid hand-designed rules that either over-call the slow system or call it at suboptimal times.
AI.method: Formulates slow-system invocation as a resource-aware sequential decision problem. ASSCG uses an RWKV backbone for efficient long-horizon gating and is trained with supervised fine-tuning + GRPO-style compute-aware reinforcement fine-tuning, making frame-level Query/Cache/Drop decisions.
AI.result: On nuPlan Hard20 (AsyncDriver): score 67.28 (+2.28), 60% latency reduction. On NAVSIM (RecogDrive-based): 91.4 PDMS (+0.6), 25% speed increase.
AI.conclusion: ASSCG effectively replaces hand-designed triggering rules with a learned gating policy, improving both performance and efficiency in fast-slow autonomous driving planners.

title: One Body, Two Minds: Variable Autonomy Approach for a Co-embodied Robotic Hand
summary:
Assistive robotic systems face a fundamental trade-off: fully autonomous systems lack user agency, while fully user-controlled systems demand continuous cognitive effort. Existing shared autonomy approaches blend human and robot commands but are mostly deployed in separate physical bodies. We introduce co-embodiment with variable autonomy, where human and robot share a single physical body and operate at different autonomy levels across task phases, from mutual autonomy during object search and grasping to human-dominant control during actuation.
  We present a co-embodied, wearable robotic hand that has its own ``mind'' and operates with variable autonomy levels. A learning-from-demonstration visuomotor diffusion policy enables autonomous grasping when the user positions the hand near known objects. Once grasped, the system signals completion and the human can actuate the grasped tool (drill, spray bottle, infrared thermometer, lighter, and ice-cream scoop) via hands-free head gestures. The human retains veto authority at all times through a release gesture that returns the system to the initial phase. Unlike blended autonomy, where control is continuously negotiated, our co-embodied approach consists of variable autonomy from full human control to full independent actions while maintaining physical coupling, realizing a one body, two minds paradigm.
  In a user study with 44 participants performing five bimanual tasks, users rapidly adapted to this ``two minds'' paradigm: completion times improved by 23.3% across trials ($p < 0.001$, Cohen's $d = 0.94$), the best-performing policy variant reached a 93.6% task success rate, and acceptance ratings were high (5.70/7 overall impression, 5.52/7 daily use willingness). This work establishes co-embodiment with variable autonomy as a viable approach for assistive robotics, enabling human-robot collaboration through co-embodiment.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a co-embodied wearable robotic hand with variable autonomy, where human and robot share a single physical body and operate at different autonomy levels across task phases. The system uses a learning-from-demonstration visuomotor diffusion policy for autonomous grasping, followed by human-dominant control via head gestures. A user study with 44 participants showed 23.3% improvement in completion times, 93.6% task success rate, and high acceptance ratings (5.70/7).
AI.motivation: Assistive robotic systems face a fundamental trade-off between full autonomy (lack of user agency) and full user control (continuous cognitive effort). Existing shared autonomy approaches blend human and robot commands but are mostly deployed in separate physical bodies.
AI.method: The authors propose co-embodiment with variable autonomy where human and robot share a single physical body (wearable robotic hand). A learning-from-demonstration visuomotor diffusion policy enables autonomous grasping near known objects. After grasping, the system signals completion and the human actuates tools via hands-free head gestures. The user retains veto authority through a release gesture. This realizes a "one body, two minds" paradigm.
AI.result: 44 participants performed five bimanual tasks. Completion times improved by 23.3% across trials (p < 0.001, Cohen's d = 0.94). Best policy variant achieved 93.6% task success rate. User acceptance was high with 5.70/7 overall impression and 5.52/7 daily use willingness.
AI.conclusion: Co-embodiment with variable autonomy is established as a viable approach for assistive robotics, enabling effective human-robot collaboration through co-embodiment.

title: WOLF-VLA: Whole-Body Humanoid Optimal Locomotion Framework for Vision-Language-Action Learning
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently demonstrated strong generalization in robotic manipulation, yet their applicability to whole-body, contact-rich humanoid locomotion remains severely underexplored due to data scarcity, the absence of dynamically consistent demonstrations, and the difficulty of encoding optimality and safety in learning-based pipelines. This work introduces a unified framework WOLF-VLA that integrates whole-body optimal-control (OC) motion synthesis with large-scale multi-modal dataset to train VLAs capable of generating humanoid locomotion policies directly from natural-language instructions. We construct a comprehensive dataset of dynamically feasible humanoid trajectories across six locomotion-related task families, each parameterized by environmental variations, object colors, placements, and visual distractors. We train a VLA model using the collected joint trajectories, ego-centric visual observations and natural language instruction, yielding a policy that exhibits strong reasoning and robustness to initial-condition variability, and competitive performance across several tasks and environment settings. A systematic ablation study demonstrates the impact of each modality on the model performance. The full dataset, model checkpoints, and benchmarking simulation suite will be openly released, establishing a reproducible dynamically consistent benchmark for whole-body humanoid locomotion rich VLA control and enabling future research in scalable transfer of instruction-driven locomotion policies.

AI.tldr: WOLF-VLA integrates whole-body optimal-control motion synthesis with multi-modal datasets to train Vision-Language-Action models that generate humanoid locomotion policies from natural language instructions, demonstrating strong reasoning, robustness, and competitive performance across six locomotion task families.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models show strong generalization in robotic manipulation but are underexplored for whole-body, contact-rich humanoid locomotion due to data scarcity, lack of dynamically consistent demonstrations, and difficulty encoding optimality/safety in learning-based pipelines.
AI.method: (1) Constructs a comprehensive dataset of dynamically feasible humanoid trajectories across six locomotion task families using whole-body optimal-control motion synthesis; (2) Trains a VLA model on joint trajectories, ego-centric visual observations, and natural language instructions.
AI.result: The trained policy exhibits strong reasoning, robustness to initial-condition variability, and competitive performance across various tasks and environment settings. Ablation studies show impact of each modality on model performance.
AI.conclusion: The open-sourced dataset, model checkpoints, and benchmarking suite establish a reproducible dynamically consistent benchmark for whole-body humanoid locomotion VLA control, enabling future research in scalable transfer of instruction-driven locomotion policies.

title: Event-Adaptive Motion Planning with Distilled Vision-Language Model in Safety-Critical Situations
comment: 8 pages, 8 figures, 4 tables. Accepted by IROS 2026
summary: Robot navigation in safety-critical scenarios faces significant challenges from unforeseen semantic events, where collisions arise primarily from the unpredictable behaviors of dynamic agents rather than unseen objects. While large vision-language models (VLMs) offer remarkable capabilities in commonsense reasoning, frequently invoking them within the continuous control loop introduces severe computational latency, fundamentally destabilizing physical execution. To address these challenges, we propose event-adaptive motion planning (EAMP), an efficient framework for VLM-based robot navigation. Specifically, a prompt-configurable semantic event trigger (PC-SET) selectively activates semantic intervention by continuously monitoring short temporal clips for behavioral anomalies. Upon triggering, an event-triggered distilled SemNav-VLM, fine-tuned via physically verified semantic distillation, maps detected anomalies into discrete strategy-level decisions. Subsequently, a semantic model predictive control (SMPC) module translates these strategies into dynamic reconfigurations of optimization objectives and geometric references. Extensive experiments in safety-critical logistics scenarios demonstrate that EAMP effectively aligns high-level reasoning with low-level control, significantly improving dynamic safety margins over existing baselines while preserving real-time efficiency.

AI.tldr: EAMP is an efficient VLM-based navigation framework that uses a selective semantic trigger, distilled vision-language model, and semantic model predictive control to handle unforeseen semantic events in safety-critical robot navigation, improving safety while maintaining real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Robot navigation in safety-critical scenarios faces collisions from unpredictable dynamic agent behaviors rather than static obstacles, and using large VLMs continuously causes severe computational latency that destabilizes physical execution.
AI.method: The EAMP framework has three key components: (1) a prompt-configurable semantic event trigger (PC-SET) that selectively activates based on behavioral anomaly detection, (2) an event-triggered distilled SemNav-VLM fine-tuned via physically verified semantic distillation for strategy-level decisions, and (3) a semantic model predictive control (SMPC) module that translates strategies into dynamic reconfigurations of objectives and geometric references.
AI.result: EAMP effectively aligns high-level reasoning with low-level control, significantly improves dynamic safety margins over existing baselines, and preserves real-time efficiency in safety-critical logistics scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed event-adaptive approach addresses the latency challenge of VLM-based navigation by selectively invoking semantic reasoning only when needed, achieving both safety and efficiency in dynamic environments.

title: Calousel: Extrinsic Calibration of Non-overlapping Multi-camera Systems from Pure Rotation
summary: Extrinsic calibration of multi-camera systems with non-overlapping FOVs has been a challenging problem in the robotics literature. Conventional target-based methods impose substantial target setup overhead, either deploying large calibration targets or requiring pre-measured multi-target poses. Motion-based approaches instead suffer from drift error, scale ambiguity, and motion degeneracy. Securing both accuracy and usability, we propose a novel calibration method that leverages pure rotational motion, requiring only a single static calibration board. The key idea is to make all cameras sequentially observe the same target under a shared geometric reference, even without overlapping views. To integrate these time-separated observations, we formulate the problem using a latent turntable frame and a 3D error on SE(3) within a global optimization framework. We validate the proposed method on both a controlled camera rig and a full-scale vehicle platform with heterogeneous cameras, and analyze robustness under non-ideal turntable motion. Extensive experiments show that our approach maintains competitive accuracy without specialized precision hardware, proving its strong suitability for realistic on-site deployments. Our code is publicly available here.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-camera extrinsic calibration method using pure rotational motion and a single static calibration board, achieving competitive accuracy without specialized hardware by leveraging a latent turntable frame and global SE(3) optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-camera calibration methods with non-overlapping FOVs suffer from either high setup overhead (large targets, pre-measured poses) or drift/scale ambiguity (motion-based approaches). The authors aim to bridge accuracy and usability.
AI.method: The method uses pure rotational motion to let all cameras sequentially observe the same static calibration target under a shared geometric reference. A latent turntable frame is introduced, and the problem is formulated as a global optimization with 3D error on SE(3).
AI.result: Validated on a controlled camera rig and a full-scale vehicle with heterogeneous cameras. Achieves competitive accuracy without precision hardware, and remains robust under non-ideal turntable motion.
AI.conclusion: The approach is strongly suitable for realistic on-site deployments, balancing accuracy and usability without requiring specialized hardware.

title: Learning to Adapt: Reptile-D-Learning for Robust and Efficient Control Under Parametric Uncertainty
summary: Learning-based Lyapunov Control (LLC) provides formal stability guarantees for nonlinear systems, but its validity relies heavily on accurate system models. Parameter variations and uncertainties may invalidate stability constraints, leading to costly retraining. Although D-learning can estimate Lyapunov derivatives without relying on explicit dynamics models, it remains limited by single-task dynamics and degrades under large parameter shifts. We propose Reptile-D-learning, a framework that leverages the Reptile meta-learning algorithm to capture shared dynamical structures across systems with different parameters, thereby learning a generalizable Lyapunov network initialization and a high-performance controller. Experiments on multiple nonlinear control systems demonstrate that Reptile-D-learning significantly improves both generalization and rapid adaptation to unseen parameter configurations.

AI.tldr: Reptile-D-learning uses meta-learning to train Lyapunov controllers that quickly adapt to new system parameters without full retraining.
AI.motivation: LLC requires accurate system models and fails under parameter variations, while D-learning is limited to single-task dynamics and degrades under large parameter shifts – both need costly retraining.
AI.method: Combines Reptile meta-learning with D-learning to learn a generalizable Lyapunov network initialization and controller across systems with different parameters.
AI.result: On multiple nonlinear control systems, the method significantly improves generalization and rapid adaptation to unseen parameter configurations.
AI.conclusion: Reptile-D-learning effectively captures shared dynamical structures, enabling fast adaptation and reducing the need for expensive retraining under parameter uncertainty.

title: Power-Budgeted Underwater Vehicle Control via Constrained Reinforcement Learning
summary: Underwater vehicles operate from a fixed onboard energy budget that propulsion rapidly depletes, so a controller that completes its task while drawing less thruster power directly extends mission range and endurance. Reinforcement learning yields capable model-free controllers for station-keeping and trajectory tracking, but optimizing task accuracy alone drives the policy toward oscillatory, energy-wasting actuation. The established remedy subtracts an energy penalty from the reward, yet this sets the task-power trade-off through a single weight with no physical units: a target power level cannot be specified, the weight must be re-tuned for every vehicle and task, and a mismatched weight can even raise power. This paper instead formulates energy-efficient underwater control as a constrained Markov decision process in which average thruster power is subject to an explicit budget, solved with a PPO-Lagrangian algorithm. The power level is set by declaring a budget in physical units, and a single dual variable is updated online to meet it for each vehicle and task, without manual weight search. Across three vehicles and four tasks in the MarineGym simulator, the energy-constrained policy draws the least power in all twelve settings, reducing it by 14--65\% (up to 64.9\%) over a task-only baseline and below an energy-reward baseline everywhere, while remaining the smoothest in ten settings and preserving task accuracy except in one deliberately power-limited regime. Imposing energy as an explicit constraint thus offers a tuning-free route to energy-efficient underwater control that needs no per-vehicle, per-task weight search.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using a constrained Markov decision process with PPO-Lagrangian to enforce an explicit power budget (in physical units) for energy-efficient underwater vehicle control, achieving 14-65% power reduction across three vehicles and four tasks without manual weight tuning.
AI.motivation: Underwater vehicles have limited onboard energy, and standard RL controllers optimized solely for task accuracy tend to produce oscillatory, energy-wasting actuation. Existing reward-penalty approaches require manual weight tuning with no physical interpretability and can even increase power draw if mismatched.
AI.method: Formulates energy-efficient control as a constrained Markov decision process (CMDP) with an explicit average thruster power budget, solved via PPO-Lagrangian where a single dual variable is updated online to enforce the power constraint for each vehicle and task without manual weight search.
AI.result: Across 3 vehicles and 4 tasks in MarineGym, the energy-constrained policy reduced power by 14-65% (up to 64.9%) over a task-only baseline, outperformed an energy-reward baseline everywhere, remained the smoothest in 10/12 settings, and preserved task accuracy in all but one deliberately power-limited regime.
AI.conclusion: Imposing energy as an explicit constraint provides a tuning-free, physically interpretable approach to energy-efficient underwater control that eliminates per-vehicle, per-task weight search.

title: SA-LIVO: Efficient LiDAR-Inertial-Visual Odometry with Subspace-Aware Degeneracy Handling
summary: Tightly coupled LiDAR-visual-inertial odometry (LIVO) fuses precise geometric depth with complementary visual measurements, yet its exteroceptive sensors face independent failure modes: LiDAR degenerates when scan geometry is under-constrained, while visual measurements degrade under adverse illumination or texture absence. Existing countermeasures, including binary degeneracy detection, covariance inflation, and scene-level quality gating, operate at the modality level and leave the direction-dependent structure of the joint information matrix unaddressed. Consequently, visual residuals enter pose directions where LiDAR is well-constrained, while in deficient directions visual compensation disperses across the full state space rather than concentrating where needed. We propose SA-LIVO, a LiDAR-inertial-visual odometry system addressing these limitations through direction-selective fusion and information-efficient processing. The Subspace-Aware Information Fusion (SAIF) framework eigendecomposes the joint LiDAR-visual information matrix and applies a linear-clamp soft gate per eigendirection, attenuating degenerate directions while preserving observable ones at full strength. LiDAR and visual residuals are then jointly optimized in one InEKF loop at a shared linearization point. Since visual information contributes only where LiDAR is deficient, photometric Jacobians are assembled once before the loop and reused across iterations, avoiding the per-iteration cost of conventional iterated filters. Experiments on 29 sequences from three benchmarks (HILTI'22, New College, Oxford Spires) and concurrent-degradation scenarios show accuracy competitive with the strongest baselines and bounded drift where competing systems diverge. SA-LIVO averages 12.3 ms per frame on a laptop CPU and 26.8 ms on an embedded ARM board without GPU, with 3.6-6.3x lower peak memory. The code will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: SA-LIVO proposes a subspace-aware information fusion (SAIF) framework that eigendecomposes the joint LiDAR-visual information matrix and applies direction-selective soft gating, enabling visual information to only compensate where LiDAR is deficient, achieving competitive accuracy with lower latency and memory.
AI.motivation: LiDAR and visual sensors have complementary but independent failure modes (LiDAR degenerates under under-constrained geometry, visual fails under poor lighting/texture). Existing methods address degeneracy at the modality level (binary detection, covariance inflation, quality gating) but ignore the direction-dependent structure of the joint information matrix, causing visual residuals to enter well-constrained LiDAR directions and visual compensation to disperse instead of concentrating where needed.
AI.method: SA-LIVO uses the Subspace-Aware Information Fusion (SAIF) framework: it eigendecomposes the joint LiDAR-visual information matrix, applies a linear-clamp soft gate per eigendirection to attenuate degenerate directions while preserving well-observable ones, jointly optimizes residuals in one InEKF loop at a shared linearization point, and reuses photometric Jacobians across iterations to reduce per-iteration cost.
AI.result: Tested on 29 sequences across HILTI'22, New College, Oxford Spires benchmarks and concurrent-degradation scenarios. Achieves accuracy competitive with strongest baselines and bounded drift where others diverge. Runs at 12.3 ms/frame on laptop CPU and 26.8 ms on embedded ARM without GPU, with 3.6-6.3x lower peak memory.
AI.conclusion: SA-LIVO demonstrates that direction-selective information fusion effectively handles degenerate scenarios in LIVO, improving efficiency (lower latency and memory) while maintaining state-of-the-art accuracy, and will be open-sourced.

title: Learning Asynchronous Upper-body Task-space Trajectory Tracking Policy for Humanoid Robots
summary: High-level humanoid planners often output sparse task-space, low-rate trajectories, whereas whole-body controllers run at high frequency. This creates temporal asynchrony between the planning and execution, and structural incompleteness for full-body control. We propose an asynchronous upper body task-space tracking framework for humanoids. A student policy is initialized by teacher-student distillation, conditioned on the full cached future trajectory and an execution-time index, and trained with a sliding-window global reward to reduce frame drift without explicit frame estimation. For task-specific post-training, an MPC module completes sparse references into floating-base and upper-body guidance, while action- and FK level self-guidance constrain policy drift. Simulation and Unitree G1 hardware experiments show improved tracking under low update rates, stronger performance than synchronous and decoupled baselines, and safer adaptation to out-of-distribution motions.

AI.tldr: A framework for asynchronous upper-body task-space tracking in humanoids, using teacher-student distillation, sliding-window reward, and MPC-based post-training to handle temporal and structural gaps between sparse planners and high-frequency controllers.
AI.motivation: Address temporal asynchrony (low-rate sparse planning vs. high-rate whole-body control) and structural incompleteness (task-space plans lacking full-body references) in humanoid control.
AI.method: Teacher-student distillation initializes a policy conditioned on cached future trajectory and execution index; sliding-window global reward reduces frame drift; MPC module and self-guidance (action & FK level) enable task-specific post-training.
AI.result: Improved tracking under low update rates, outperforms synchronous and decoupled baselines, safer OOD motion adaptation; validated in simulation and on Unitree G1 hardware.
AI.conclusion: The asynchronous tracking framework effectively bridges sparse planning and high-frequency control, enabling robust and safe humanoid whole-body motion execution.

title: Stage-Aware and Roughness-Constrained Diffusion Policy for Multi-Stage Robotic Polishing
summary: Polishing is a critical finishing process in high-end manufacturing fields such as aerospace, where surface quality directly affects the service performance and reliability of components. Robotic imitation learning provides a flexible solution for such tasks, but current methods remain limited in industrial polishing because of long-horizon dependencies, uncertain stage transitions, and the difficulty of modeling and regulating coupled process parameters. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Stage-Aware and Roughness-Constrained Diffusion Policy (SRDP) for robotic polishing. SRDP infers the process-stage posterior from multimodal observation histories and uses it to condition the shared reverse denoising process, enabling stage-consistent action generation without external stage labels during execution. Furthermore, a roughness-oriented process-constrained diffusion sampling method is incorporated to generate constrained feed speed and normal contact force under stage-wise preset spindle speeds, thereby improving process consistency and physical feasibility. Systematic experiments are conducted on two representative scenarios, namely spacecraft cabin coating-surface polishing and inner-cavity structural surface finishing. Comparisons with advanced baselines, ablation studies, and real-robot validations comprehensively evaluate the proposed method. The results show that SRD improves stage-transition stability, process-parameter consistency, and final surface quality across different polishing scenarios.

AI.tldr: SRDP improves robotic polishing by using stage-aware diffusion policy and roughness-constrained sampling for better surface quality and process consistency.
AI.motivation: Current robotic imitation learning for polishing suffers from long-horizon dependencies, uncertain stage transitions, and difficulty in modeling coupled process parameters.
AI.method: Proposes Stage-Aware and Roughness-Constrained Diffusion Policy (SRDP) that infers process-stage posteriors from multimodal observation histories, conditions shared reverse denoising, and uses roughness-oriented constrained diffusion sampling with stage-wise preset spindle speeds.
AI.result: SRDP improves stage-transition stability, process-parameter consistency, and final surface quality across spacecraft cabin coating and inner-cavity structural surface polishing scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Stage-aware and roughness-constrained diffusion policy effectively addresses key limitations in robotic polishing imitation learning, demonstrating superior performance in real-robot validations.

title: StairMaster: Learning to Conquer Risky Hollow Stairs for Agile Quadrupedal Robots
summary:
Climbing hollow stairs remains a challenging problem for quadruped robots due to the high risk of leg trapping, severe depth sparsity, and high-frequency depth-sensing noise. In this paper, we propose StairMaster, a novel three-stage reinforcement learning framework for stable locomotion on such extreme discontinuous terrains. Our architecture integrates a Cross-Attention mechanism to extract structural features from noisy depth data, alongside a Spatial-aware Recurrent Unit (SRU) that maintains robust spatio-temporal memory to mitigate perception blind spots. To bridge the sim-to-real gap in depth perception, we propose a high-fidelity sim-to-real depth sensor modeling pipeline that faithfully replicates real-world sensor artifacts. Additionally, we employ a 3D waypoint-guided active perception reward for proactive sensing, alongside hollow gap kinematic and stair edge penalties to ensure precise foothold placement. We successfully deployed StairMaster on a Unitree Go2 robot, demonstrating its ability to conquer hollow stairs with an unprecedented incline of up to 55$^\circ$ through zero-shot transfer. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first RL-based policy to achieve such steep hollow stair climbing in real-world environments.
  Project Website: https://sivan666666.github.io/StairMaster/.

AI.tldr: StairMaster is a reinforcement learning framework enabling quadruped robots to climb steep hollow stairs (up to 55°) via cross-attention, spatial-temporal memory, and realistic depth sensor simulation, deployed zero-shot on a Unitree Go2.
AI.motivation: Hollow stair climbing is extremely challenging for quadrupeds due to leg trapping risks, sparse depth data, and noise. Existing RL methods fail on such extreme discontinuous terrains.
AI.method: Three-stage RL framework with Cross-Attention for structural feature extraction from noisy depth, Spatial-aware Recurrent Unit (SRU) for spatio-temporal memory, high-fidelity depth sensor sim-to-real pipeline, 3D waypoint-guided active perception reward, and kinematic/gap penalties for precise foothold placement.
AI.result: Achieved zero-shot deployment on Unitree Go2, climbing hollow stairs up to 55° incline — the first RL policy to accomplish such steep hollow stair climbing in real-world environments.
AI.conclusion: StairMaster demonstrates that combining attention mechanisms, spatial-temporal memory, and accurate sensor simulation can bridge the sim-to-real gap for extreme terrain locomotion, setting a new benchmark for hollow stair climbing.

title: MIL-LC: A Robust Magnetometer-Inertial-LiDAR Fusion Multimodal Localization Framework
summary: Localization in challenging environments, such as GNSS-denied, geometrically repetitive, or textureless scenes commonly found in offices, hotels, and underground parking facilities, remains an open problem for reliable autonomous mobile robot (AMR) deployment. Single-modality localization methods are inherently limited by the constraints of individual sensors. Although multimodal fusion frameworks have shown improved robustness, most existing approaches still rely heavily on geometric or texture features, or on infrastructure-based beacons, which increase installation and maintenance costs while reducing deployment flexibility. Recently, ambient magnetic field (AMF)-based localization has attracted growing attention because it does not depend on geometric or texture features, nor does it require additional infrastructure, making it a promising complementary modality for AMR localization. However, existing studies have only explored such fusion in pedestrian scenarios using smartphone-mounted sensor suites, and practical solutions for AMR systems remain largely unexplored. To address this gap, this article proposes a magnetometer-inertial-LiDAR fused multimodal localization framework with a custom-designed sensor suite, termed MIL-LC, which provides reliable localization even when LiDAR suffers from geometric degeneration or when the magnetic map changes during long-term deployment. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world environments demonstrate that the proposed MIL-LC framework achieves robust and accurate localization performance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MIL-LC, a multimodal localization framework fusing magnetometer, inertial sensors, and LiDAR, designed for challenging environments (GNSS-denied, textureless, repetitive scenes). It addresses the gap in applying ambient magnetic field-based fusion to autonomous mobile robots (AMRs). Results show robust and accurate localization in both simulation and real-world tests.
AI.motivation: Existing single-modality and multimodal localization methods fail in GNSS-denied, repetitive, or textureless environments, and most fusion approaches still depend on geometric/texture features or expensive infrastructure. Ambient magnetic field (AMF)-based localization offers a promising infrastructure-free and feature-independent complementary modality, but its application in AMR systems is unexplored.
AI.method: The authors propose MIL-LC, a magnetometer-inertial-LiDAR fused multimodal localization framework with a custom-designed sensor suite, which fuses ambient magnetic field, inertial, and LiDAR data to maintain reliable localization even when LiDAR suffers from geometric degeneration or magnetic maps change over time.
AI.result: Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate robust and accurate localization performance of the proposed MIL-LC framework in challenging environments.
AI.conclusion: The MIL-LC framework effectively bridges the gap of AMF-based multimodal localization for AMRs, providing a reliable solution for challenging environments without relying on geometric/texture features or additional infrastructure.

title: Beyond a Shadow of a Doubt: Close Proximity Geometry Reconstruction Using FMCW Radar Shadow Effects
summary: Reliable perception in adverse conditions remains challenging for autonomous systems, as cameras and LiDAR degrade in poor lighting or weather. Millimetre-wave FMCW radar is robust to such conditions, but its elevation collapse limits geometric reasoning. We observe that vehicle chassis occlude radar rays and form a distinctive geometric shadow, and its consistency can enable us to infer useful information about objects whose returns intersect this shadow. Motivated by this observation, we propose a method to recover the 3D, in-plane inclination of nearby slender vertical objects from this cue. The object inclination is retrieved without assumptions about the wider scene, but through an analytical, closed-form mapping between its radar return boundaries and the opening angle. Validation in simulation and experimentation on a Navtech CTS350-X radar shows that inclinations can be estimated under practical conditions, with segmentation of the object in the radar scan emerging as the main bottleneck. This work highlights chassis shadows as a novel geometric cue, extending the role of 2D rotating radar beyond localisation and toward 3D scene reconstruction.

AI.tldr: The paper exploits vehicle chassis-induced radar shadows as a geometric cue to estimate the 3D inclination of nearby vertical objects using 2D rotating FMCW radar, validated in simulation and real-world experiments.
AI.motivation: Reliable perception in adverse conditions (poor lighting/weather) is challenging for autonomous systems because cameras and LiDAR degrade, while FMCW radar is robust but suffers from elevation collapse (lack of 3D information). The authors aim to recover 3D geometric information from 2D radar by leveraging a previously overlooked geometric cue.
AI.method: The method uses the observation that vehicle chassis blocks radar rays, creating a consistent geometric shadow. By analyzing the radar return boundaries of slender vertical objects intersecting this shadow, a closed-form analytical mapping computes the object's in-plane inclination without scene assumptions. Validation uses simulation and a Navtech CTS350-X radar.
AI.result: Inclinations can be estimated under practical conditions; the main bottleneck is segmentation of the object in the radar scan. The approach works in both simulation and real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: Chassis shadows are introduced as a novel geometric cue for 2D rotating radar, extending its capability beyond localization toward 3D scene reconstruction.

title: TacVerse: A Multi-Sensor Dataset and Benchmark for Cross-Sensor Vision-Based Tactile Perception
summary: Vision-based tactile sensors (VBTSs) enable robots to infer contact geometry and force-related cues by imaging deformation through an internal camera, yet generalisation across sensor designs remains poorly understood. We present TacVerse, a multi-sensor dataset and benchmark for cross-sensor vision-based tactile perception. The dataset contains 106,800 tactile images from seven VBTSs and supports three downstream tasks: shape classification, grating classification, and force regression. Experiments are conducted under three settings: within-sensor training, zero-shot cross-sensor transfer, and few-shot adaptation. Strong within-sensor performance across all tasks indicates that the collected tactile observations are informative for the target objectives. Direct cross-sensor transfer, however, leads to substantial degradation. Shape classification is comparatively robust, whereas grating classification and force regression are more sensitive to sensor shift. Few-shot adaptation for force regression consistently improves performance on unseen target sensors but does not fully close the gap to within-sensor upper bounds. A representation study further shows that MAE (Masked Autoencoder) pretraining provides the most consistent gains across tasks and sensors. TacVerse provides a controlled testbed for studying sensor shift, data-efficient adaptation, and self-supervised learning in tactile perception.

AI.tldr: TacVerse is a multi-sensor tactile dataset (106,800 images from 7 VBTSs) for cross-sensor perception tasks. While within-sensor performance is strong, direct cross-sensor transfer degrades significantly. Few-shot adaptation helps but doesn't match within-sensor bounds. MAE pretraining offers the most consistent improvements across tasks and sensors.
AI.motivation: Generalization across different vision-based tactile sensor designs remains poorly understood, limiting the reusability of models trained on one sensor for another.
AI.method: The authors collected 106,800 tactile images from seven different VBTSs, supporting shape classification, grating classification, and force regression. They evaluated under within-sensor, zero-shot cross-sensor transfer, and few-shot adaptation settings, and investigated MAE pretraining for representation learning.
AI.result: Within-sensor performance is strong across tasks. Zero-shot cross-sensor transfer degrades substantially—shape classification is most robust, while grating classification and force regression are more sensitive. Few-shot adaptation improves force regression but doesn't reach within-sensor upper bounds. MAE pretraining yields the most consistent gains.
AI.conclusion: TacVerse serves as a controlled testbed for studying sensor shift, data-efficient adaptation, and self-supervised learning in tactile perception, revealing that cross-sensor generalization remains a significant challenge.

title: A 3D-Printable Dataset for Fair Testing and Comparisons of Tactile Sensors
summary: Existing texture datasets for tactile sensing primarily consist of sensor readings from a specific sensor interacting with available surfaces/objects rather than describing the textures themselves, limiting fair comparison between tactile sensors and hindering reproducible research. In this work, we introduce a 3D-printable dataset of mathematically defined textures designed to be fabricated reliably across different printers and filament types. The dataset consists of six parametrically generated surface patterns derived from combinations of sine-wave and Fourier-based functions, giving controlled variation in spatial frequency, amplitude, and directional structure. We evaluate the reproducibility of these textures across three popular 3D printers and multiple filament types by measuring variance in images captured using an optical TacTip sensor under controlled contact conditions. Our results show that print quality, particularly peak sharpness and stringing, affects tactile variance, with higher-end printers producing significantly more consistent signatures. Classification experiments using neural networks and PCA-based models further demonstrate that high-quality prints support strong within-printer generalisation, while cross-printer generalisation remains challenging due to geometric inconsistencies. This work establishes the first openly available, physically reproducible 3D-printed texture benchmark, providing a foundation for fair comparison of tactile sensors.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a 3D-printable dataset of mathematically defined textures for tactile sensing, enabling fair comparison across different sensors and printers by controlling surface geometry via parametric functions.
AI.motivation: Existing tactile texture datasets are sensor-specific and lack reproducibility, hindering fair sensor comparisons and reproducible research in tactile sensing.
AI.method: Six parametric surface patterns were designed using sine-wave and Fourier-based functions. These were 3D-printed across three printers and multiple filaments, then evaluated using an optical TacTip sensor under controlled conditions.
AI.result: Higher-end printers produced more consistent tactile signatures; within-printer generalization was strong, but cross-printer generalization remains difficult due to geometric inconsistencies.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes the first openly available, physically reproducible 3D-printed texture benchmark, providing a foundation for fair tactile sensor comparison.

title: DeformGen: Dynamics-Based Topology Augmentation for Deformable Manipulation Policy Learning
summary: Demonstration augmentation is proposed for cost-efficient data acquisition, but existing methods are fundamentally limited in deformable manipulation due to two challenges: (1) the state space is high-dimensional with physics-induced constraints, making valid configurations impossible to reach via low-dimensional pose perturbations; and (2) trajectory transfer is non-equivariant, as material points no longer move rigidly together under deformation. We present DeformGen, a dynamics-based augmentation framework that achieves topological diversity for deformable objects. For the state challenge, DeformGen expands the valid state distribution by applying localized physical disturbances and forward-simulating the dynamics to obtain topology-coherent, physically plausible deformable states. For the trajectory challenge, DeformGen transfers source manipulation trajectories via deformation-field warping, which lifts per-particle displacements into a continuous spatial function to adapt the end-effector trajectory consistently with the deformed geometry. In this way, our method jointly augments the state distribution and its associated manipulation behavior. Experiments on high-fidelity deformable manipulation benchmarks show that DeformGen generally improves policy learning compared with training on the original demonstrations alone and with rigid-style augmentation baselines.

AI.tldr: DeformGen is a dynamics-based data augmentation framework that addresses two key challenges in deformable manipulation—high-dimensional state constraints and non-equivariant trajectory transfer—by applying localized physical disturbances with forward simulation for state diversity and using deformation-field warping for consistent trajectory transfer, significantly improving policy learning.
AI.motivation: Existing demonstration augmentation methods fail in deformable manipulation due to (1) the high-dimensional, physics-constrained state space where simple pose perturbations cannot produce valid configurations, and (2) the non-equivariant nature of trajectory transfer under deformation, where material points do not move rigidly together.
AI.method: DeformGen augments data via (1) localized physical disturbances + forward dynamics simulation to generate physically plausible, topology-coherent deformable states, and (2) deformation-field warping that lifts per-particle displacements into continuous spatial functions to adapt end-effector trajectories consistently with deformed geometry.
AI.result: Experiments on high-fidelity deformable manipulation benchmarks show DeformGen generally improves policy learning over training on original demonstrations alone and over rigid-style augmentation baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dynamics-based augmentation with deformation-aware trajectory warping is an effective approach for achieving topological diversity and improving policy learning in deformable object manipulation.

title: DSP-SLAM++: A Unified Framework for Multi-Class, High-Fidelity Object SLAM in the Wild
summary: Existing object-aware SLAM systems force a trade-off between real-time performance, multi-class support, and the generation of high-fidelity, semantically coherent object models. To address this trade-off, we present DSP-SLAM++, which extends the DSP-SLAM framework with an asynchronous mapping pipeline for real-time performance and dedicated sensor fusion adaptations for a monocular fisheye-LiDAR suite. Experiments demonstrate that our system generates fine-grained, geometrically-complete shapes for multiple object classes while eliminating severe mapping thread bottlenecks by reducing maximum object processing latency by up to 70\% compared to the state-of-the-art baseline, enabling robust, real-time performance on a challenging 25 Hz multi-class datasets. This work makes high-fidelity, multi-class object SLAM more practical for real-world applications like autonomous driving and robotic manipulation by enabling its use on platforms with common fisheye-LiDAR sensor setups. The open-source code is available at: [github.com/AUBVRL/DSP-SLAMpp].

AI.tldr: DSP-SLAM++ enables real-time, multi-class object-aware SLAM with high-fidelity object models by using an asynchronous mapping pipeline and fisheye-LiDAR fusion, cutting object processing latency by up to 70%.
AI.motivation: Existing object-aware SLAM systems trade off real-time performance, multi-class support, and high-fidelity semantically coherent object model generation, making them impractical for real-world applications.
AI.method: Extends DSP-SLAM with an asynchronous mapping pipeline for real-time performance and dedicated sensor fusion adaptations for a monocular fisheye-LiDAR sensor suite.
AI.result: Generates fine-grained, geometrically-complete shapes for multiple object classes, reduces maximum object processing latency by up to 70% compared to the state-of-the-art baseline, and achieves robust real-time performance on 25 Hz multi-class datasets.
AI.conclusion: This work makes high-fidelity, multi-class object SLAM more practical for real-world applications such as autonomous driving and robotic manipulation, especially on platforms with common fisheye-LiDAR sensor setups.

title: From Rubble Simulation to Active Magnetic Mapping: Quantum Sensing for Disaster Response
summary:
Locating survivors of building collapses within the first 72 hours is a critical challenge in disaster response, and existing sensing modalities provide only partial information about the structure beneath the rubble.
  This paper proposes drone-based quantum magnetometry as a complementary modality and develops a simulation pipeline spanning rubble physics, sensor-array deployment, and active spatial reconstruction. We use Unreal Engine to generate a steel-reinforced concrete parking-garage collapse and compute the induced magnetic field via a per-triangle dipole approximation, establishing that meaningful magnetic structure is recoverable in the sub-pT to sub-nT range from roughly 1 m above the roofline. Then, we feed sparse multi-sensor samples into a Gaussian Process Regression back-end driven by Bayesian active sampling and validate the pipeline across multiple independent collapse realizations; a three-sensor array optimizes the trade-off between gradient resolution and UAV payload constraints, and active sampling reaches peak structural correlation in roughly $100$ samples. Together, these results indicate that quantum-grade sensing could become a useful tool for drone-based structural analysis and potentially void detection in collapsed buildings.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes drone-based quantum magnetometry for locating survivors in collapsed buildings, using simulation pipelines with Unreal Engine and Bayesian active sampling to show feasibility of sub-pT magnetic sensing from above rubble.
AI.motivation: Locating survivors within the critical first 72 hours after building collapses remains extremely challenging, as existing sensing modalities provide only partial structural information beneath rubble.
AI.method: The authors build a simulation pipeline using Unreal Engine to generate a steel-reinforced concrete parking-garage collapse, compute induced magnetic fields via per-triangle dipole approximation, and feed sparse multi-sensor samples into Gaussian Process Regression with Bayesian active sampling.
AI.result: Meaningful magnetic structure is recoverable in the sub-pT to sub-nT range from ~1 m above roofline; a three-sensor array optimally balances gradient resolution and UAV payload; active sampling reaches peak structural correlation in ~100 samples.
AI.conclusion: Quantum-grade magnetometry could become a useful tool for drone-based structural analysis and void detection in collapsed buildings.

title: Mixture-of-Experts RL for Fault-Tolerant Legged Locomotion
summary: Legged robots deployed in planetary exploration and other remote environments must maintain reliable locomotion despite actuator failures and challenging terrain conditions. Although reinforcement learning has achieved strong results in legged locomotion, monolithic policies can struggle to efficiently represent the diverse control strategies required to compensate for different fault conditions. In this work, we propose a fault-aware modular control architecture that explicitly leverages fault-diagnosis information to activate specialized control experts associated with distinct actuator failure modes. Experimental results show that explicit fault-conditioned modular policies consistently outperform monolithic policies of comparable size, achieving higher locomotion performance across failure scenarios. Moreover, the proposed modular architecture retains competitive performance even under significantly reduced network capacity, highlighting its suitability for compute-constrained robotic platforms, such as those typically employed in space applications. The code associated with this work is available at: https://github.com/iit-DLSLab/fault-locomotion-isaaclab.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a fault-aware modular control architecture for legged robots that uses fault-diagnosis information to activate specialized control experts for different actuator failure modes, outperforming monolithic policies especially under compute-constrained conditions.
AI.motivation: Legged robots in planetary exploration need reliable locomotion despite actuator failures, but monolithic reinforcement learning policies struggle to efficiently represent diverse control strategies for different fault conditions.
AI.method: A fault-aware modular control architecture that leverages fault-diagnosis information to activate specialized control experts associated with distinct actuator failure modes.
AI.result: Explicit fault-conditioned modular policies consistently outperform monolithic policies of comparable size across failure scenarios, and retain competitive performance even under significantly reduced network capacity.
AI.conclusion: The modular architecture is more suitable for compute-constrained robotic platforms (e.g., space applications) than monolithic policies, and code is open-sourced.

title: A Sensorised Lattice Footplate for a Semi-Active Prosthetic Foot
summary: This paper investigates whether magnetic plantar sensing can be embedded directly inside the load-bearing compliant element of a low-cost semi-active prosthetic foot. We present a prototype integrating a sensorised 3D-printed lattice footplate, a servo-adjustable hydraulic damper, and a reduced-order ankle model. The damper is experimentally characterised to relate adjustment angle to damping coefficient. Controlled compression tests show tunable lattice stiffness, while cyclic normal loading shows that the embedded sensor tracks the testing-machine reference force, supporting plantar-force estimation without an external insole layer. Static-posture trials under approximately body-weight loading show that forefoot and rearfoot loading distributions are separable across four prescribed stance configurations, providing a preliminary check of the sensing pipeline. A feedforward damping schedule approximates the dorsiflexion trend of a reference ankle trajectory through early-to-mid stance, while exposing the expected limitation that a purely dissipative mechanism cannot generate active push-off. Together, these results demonstrate that sensing can be embedded inside the load-bearing compliant element of a prosthetic foot and used to drive semi-active damping.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that magnetic plantar sensing can be embedded inside a 3D-printed lattice footplate of a semi-active prosthetic foot, enabling plantar-force estimation and feedforward damping control without external insole layers.
AI.motivation: To determine if magnetic plantar sensing can be integrated directly into the load-bearing compliant element of a low-cost semi-active prosthetic foot, eliminating the need for external insole sensor layers.
AI.method: The authors built a prototype with a sensorised 3D-printed lattice footplate, servo-adjustable hydraulic damper, and reduced-order ankle model. They conducted controlled compression tests, cyclic loading tests, static-posture trials, and implemented a feedforward damping schedule.
AI.result: Embedded sensor tracked reference force accurately; forefoot/rearfoot loading distributions were separable across stance configurations; feedforward damping approximated dorsiflexion trends but lacked active push-off capability.
AI.conclusion: Sensing can be embedded inside the load-bearing compliant element of a prosthetic foot and used to drive semi-active damping, though a purely dissipative mechanism cannot generate active push-off.

title: Action ControlNet: A Lightweight Delay-Aware Adapter for Smooth Asynchronous Control in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for general-purpose robot manipulation, but their inference latency remains a major obstacle to stable high-frequency control. Asynchronous execution mitigates this bottleneck by overlapping policy inference with action execution, yet the next action chunk is still predicted from stale observations while the robot continues to move. Direct chunk stitching therefore introduces handoff discontinuities, action jitter, and failures in contact-rich manipulation. Existing remedies typically require either full-policy retraining or architecture-specific runtime logic. This work proposes Action ControlNet (ACNet), a lightweight delay-aware adapter that uses the executed motion suffix as a residual condition for a mostly frozen action head. ACNet leaves the pretrained backbone unchanged, introduces few trainable parameters, and remains compatible with generative action heads such as diffusion and flow matching. On Kinetix, Meta-World MT50, and a real-world SO-ARM101 platform, ACNet improves robustness under inference delay and yields smoother asynchronous trajectories than direct chunk stitching, while remaining more lightweight than full delay-conditioned retraining.

AI.tldr: ACNet is a lightweight delay-aware adapter that improves asynchronous robot control by using executed motion suffixes as residual conditions, reducing handoff discontinuities and action jitter without full retraining.
AI.motivation: Inference latency in VLA models hinders stable high-frequency robot control. Asynchronous execution causes stale observations and handoff discontinuities. Existing fixes require retraining or architecture-specific logic.
AI.method: ACNet is a delay-aware adapter that uses the executed motion suffix as a residual condition for a mostly frozen action head, leaving the pretrained backbone unchanged with few additional trainable parameters.
AI.result: On Kinetix, Meta-World MT50, and real-world SO-ARM101, ACNet improves robustness under inference delay and produces smoother trajectories than direct chunk stitching, while being more lightweight than full delay-conditioned retraining.
AI.conclusion: ACNet offers a practical, lightweight solution for asynchronous VLA control that is compatible with generative action heads and requires minimal architectural changes.

title: FORCE: Efficient VLA Reinforcement Fine-Tuning via Value-Calibrated Warm-up and Self-Distillation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are often constrained by the imitation ceiling imposed by sub-optimal data. While Reinforcement Learning (RL) fine-tuning can surpass this limit, it is notoriously sample inefficient. This challenge arises from two core issues: (1) catastrophic initial unlearning due to an unstable Q-function and (2) inefficient policy updates caused by low-quality exploration data, often forcing a reliance on costly human interventions. We introduce FORCE, a 3-stage framework that stabilizes fine-tuning by tackling both issues. FORCE first incorporates a Value-Calibrated Warm-Up phase, utilizing on-policy rollouts to mitigate the distributional shift of the Q-function. Subsequently, during the online stage, this calibrated Q-function acts as a filter for both the policy's own action proposals and expert data, ensuring only high-value actions are used for the policy update. We evaluate FORCE on various simulation and real-world tasks, and the result shows that FORCE achieves a 79% absolute improvement in success rates and outperform prior RL methods by 10%, while accelerating training by 32.5%. Critically, it mitigates the common success rate drop and achieves this robust performance without human intervention, marking a significant step towards deploying capable and autonomous robotic agents.

AI.tldr: FORCE is a 3-stage RL fine-tuning framework for VLA models that addresses catastrophic unlearning and inefficient policy updates through Value-Calibrated Warm-Up and Q-function-based action filtering, achieving 79% success rate improvement, 10% gain over prior RL methods, and 32.5% faster training without human intervention.
AI.motivation: VLA models suffer from imitation ceilings due to sub-optimal data, and RL fine-tuning is sample inefficient due to catastrophic unlearning from unstable Q-functions and inefficient policy updates from low-quality exploration data, often requiring costly human interventions.
AI.method: FORCE is a 3-stage framework: (1) Value-Calibrated Warm-Up using on-policy rollouts to stabilize the Q-function and mitigate distributional shift; (2) Online stage where the calibrated Q-function filters both the policy's own action proposals and expert data; (3) Only high-value actions are used for policy updates.
AI.result: 79% absolute improvement in success rates, 10% outperformance over prior RL methods, 32.5% training acceleration, and mitigation of success rate drop without human intervention across simulation and real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: FORCE enables stable and efficient RL fine-tuning for VLA models without human intervention, representing progress toward capable and autonomous robotic agents.

title: Emcar: Embodied Controller for Animating Robots
comment: Published in Lecture Notes in Computer Science, DOI: 10.1007/978-3-032-15501-6_11
summary: This chapter describes EMCAR, a novel software tool for programming robot motion that leverages the unique affordances of artistic practices such as puppetry and drawing to conceive, design, and program novel interactions and realize new use cases for HRI. The advantage of this no-code platform is that it expands creative applications for collaborative robots - putting robots directly in the hands of artists - and provides an inclusive environment that enables individuals with little or no technical backgrounds to engage meaningfully in collaborations and robotics research.

AI.tldr: EMCAR is a no-code tool that uses artistic practices (puppetry, drawing) to enable non-technical users to program robot motion and expand creative HRI applications.
AI.motivation: To lower the technical barrier of robot programming by leveraging artistic affordances, enabling artists and non-experts to engage in HRI research and creative applications.
AI.method: A no-code software platform (EMCAR) that integrates puppetry and drawing-based interaction paradigms for programming collaborative robot motion without traditional coding.
AI.result: Expands creative applications for collaborative robots and provides an inclusive environment for individuals with little/no technical background to participate in robotics research.
AI.conclusion: Artistic practices like puppetry and drawing can effectively serve as intuitive interfaces for robot programming, democratizing access to HRI.

title: FAR-LIO: Enabling High-Speed Autonomy through Fast, Accurate, and Robust LiDAR-Inertial Odometry
comment: 8 pages, accepted for publication at IROS2026 (IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems 2026)
summary: Robust and accurate odometry estimation is essential in modern robotics. In environments characterized by highly dynamic motion and sensor noise, odometry estimation becomes increasingly challenging. Autonomous racing combines both factors in an unstructured setting, where minimizing odometry latency is essential for stable closed-loop control. This paper introduces FAR-LIO, a highly optimized CUDA-accelerated LiDAR-inertial odometry framework developed for Fast, Accurate, and Robust performance. Our system leverages a novel CUDA-based voxel hashmap to enable parallelized nearest-neighbor search and efficient map updates. We employ a sparsity-aware Generalized Iterative Closest Point algorithm with adaptive thresholding on top of the CUDA-based voxel hashmap with adaptive density to achieve low-latency without compromising accuracy. An Extended Kalman Filter serves as a robust backend. It utilizes an upsampling and delay compensation strategy to fuse the LiDAR odometry with high-frequency IMU data, thereby ensuring a robust and smooth odometry output. We evaluate FAR-LIO across four different sensor setups, using both public datasets and data from two autonomous racecars driving at speeds of up to 250 km/h. FAR-LIO achieves an average 6.9% reduction in the positional error and 38.4% lower runtime compared to state-of-the-art baselines on target hardware using a single parameter set. This demonstrates its computational efficiency and broad applicability. To build upon our work, our code is available open-source on https://github.com/TUMFTM/FAR-LIO.

AI.tldr: FAR-LIO is a CUDA-accelerated LiDAR-inertial odometry framework that achieves fast, accurate, and robust performance by using a voxel hashmap-based parallelized nearest-neighbor search and sparsity-aware GICP with adaptive thresholding, combined with an EKF backend that fuses LiDAR odometry with high-frequency IMU data via upsampling and delay compensation.
AI.motivation: Robust and accurate odometry estimation is essential in robotics, especially in environments with highly dynamic motion and sensor noise like autonomous racing. Minimizing odometry latency is critical for stable closed-loop control, motivating the need for a faster and more robust LiDAR-inertial odometry framework.
AI.method: 1) CUDA-based voxel hashmap for parallelized nearest-neighbor search and efficient map updates. 2) Sparsity-aware Generalized Iterative Closest Point (GICP) algorithm with adaptive thresholding on top of the voxel hashmap with adaptive density. 3) Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) backend with upsampling and delay compensation to fuse LiDAR odometry with high-frequency IMU data.
AI.result: Evaluated across four sensor setups using public datasets and autonomous racecars at speeds up to 250 km/h. Achieved 6.9% reduction in positional error and 38.4% lower runtime compared to state-of-the-art baselines with a single parameter set.
AI.conclusion: FAR-LIO demonstrates computational efficiency and broad applicability for high-speed autonomous racing. Code is open-source at https://github.com/TUMFTM/FAR-LIO.

title: G2DP: Diffusion Planning with Spatio-Temporal Grid Guidance
summary: In autonomous driving, diffusion-based planners have emerged as a promising paradigm for robust motion planning in dense and interactive traffic, as they can effectively model diverse driving behaviors. However, their inherent stochasticity often requires explicit guidance during denoising to ensure safety and route adherence for robust closed-loop execution. Existing guidance typically relies on sparse, entity-centric geometric queries or post-hoc refinement, yielding limited situational awareness and fragile performance in interactive scenes. To address this issue, we propose G2DP (Grid-Guided Diffusion Planning), a diffusion-based planner that directly enforces dense environmental constraints through inference-time guidance. Specifically, G2DP constructs a differentiable spatio-temporal cost volume by fusing probabilistic future occupancy distributions with a route-progress map. By formulating this volume as a continuous safety energy functional, it injects dense gradients directly into the denoising loop, actively steering trajectory generation toward collision-free and progress-optimal regions. Extensive closed-loop evaluations show that G2DP achieves state-of-the-art performance on nuPlan, outperforming the strongest imitation-learning baseline by +7.2 points in reactive score. It further maintains top scores in zero-shot transfers to interPlan and DeepScenario benchmarks, with collision avoidance improving by +10.15 over the unguided approach on interPlan. These results demonstrate that spatio-temporal cost grids serve as an effective representation for robust guidance in diffusion-based planning.

AI.tldr: G2DP is a diffusion-based planner for autonomous driving that uses differentiable spatio-temporal cost volumes (fusing occupancy distributions with route-progress maps) to inject dense gradients during denoising, achieving state-of-the-art closed-loop performance on nuPlan (+7.2 reactive score) and strong zero-shot transfer results.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based planners rely on sparse entity-level geometric queries or post-hoc refinement for guidance, which leads to limited situational awareness and fragile performance in dense, interactive traffic scenarios.
AI.method: G2DP constructs a differentiable spatio-temporal cost volume by fusing probabilistic future occupancy distributions with a route-progress map, formulated as a continuous safety energy functional. This injects dense gradients directly into the denoising loop to steer trajectory generation toward collision-free and progress-optimal regions.
AI.result: Outperforms the strongest IL baseline by +7.2 points in reactive score on nuPlan; achieves top scores in zero-shot transfers to interPlan and DeepScenario; collision avoidance improves by +10.15 over unguided approach on interPlan.
AI.conclusion: Spatio-temporal cost grids serve as an effective representation for providing robust, dense guidance in diffusion-based planning, enabling safer and more route-adherent closed-loop driving.

title: In-Context World Modeling for Robotic Control
summary: Modern Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models often fail to generalize to novel setups, such as altered camera viewpoints or robot morphologies, because they are typically conditioned only on current observations and language instructions. By ignoring the underlying system configuration as a variable, these models implicitly assume a fixed execution context encountered during training, necessitating data-intensive fine-tuning for any new environment. In this work, we introduce In-Context World Modeling (ICWM), a framework that treats system identification as an in-context adaptation problem. ICWM enables robot policies to autonomously infer essential system variables from a short history of self-generated, task-agnostic interactions. Unlike traditional In-Context Learning that uses demonstrations to specify what task to perform, ICWM leverages the context window to understand how the system operates. By processing these interactions before task execution, the model implicitly captures the world dynamics of the current system, enabling adaptation to novel configurations without parameter updates. Extensive experiments in simulation and on real-world robot platforms demonstrate that ICWM significantly outperforms standard VLA baselines on novel camera viewpoints.

AI.tldr: ICWM enables robot policies to adapt to novel configurations (e.g., camera viewpoints, robot morphologies) by inferring system dynamics from a short history of task-agnostic interactions, without requiring parameter updates.
AI.motivation: Standard VLA models fail to generalize to novel setups (altered camera viewpoints or robot morphologies) because they assume a fixed execution context and require data-intensive fine-tuning for new environments.
AI.method: ICWM treats system identification as an in-context adaptation problem. It uses a short history of self-generated, task-agnostic interactions processed in the context window to implicitly capture world dynamics of the current system.
AI.result: Extensive simulation and real-world experiments show ICWM significantly outperforms standard VLA baselines on novel camera viewpoints.
AI.conclusion: ICWM offers a parameter-free adaptation method that improves generalization to novel configurations by leveraging in-context world modeling.

title: RoboAtlas: Contextual Active SLAM
comment: Alexander Schperberg and Shivam K. Panda made equal contribution
summary: We present RoboAtlas, a contextual Active SLAM framework that adaptively balances geometric exploration and semantic reasoning using a scalable 3D semantic mapping system, OpenRoboVox. RoboAtlas integrates frontier exploration, global semantic-map reasoning, and egocentric VLM-based reasoning through a contextual multi-armed bandit that transitions from exploration to semantically guided navigation as scene understanding improves. We evaluate the system in simulation and on a Unitree Go2 robot in large-scale real-world environments exceeding 1800 m2 with approx. 30k mapped semantic instances, achieving a 100% task success rate. On the GOAT-Bench "Val Unseen" benchmark, RoboAtlas achieves state-of-the-art performance with highest reported success rate (SR) of 90.6%, using GPT-4o, improving over the strongest prior baseline by 17.8 percentage points in SR. Using the much smaller Qwen2.5-VL-7B model, it still achieves 88.8% SR, outperforming all baselines using GPT-4o in SR, and revealing the importance of the information gained by our semantic mapping framework over simply replacing the underlying foundation model. The results demonstrate that grounding foundation models with large-scale 3D semantic maps enables robust and efficient contextual Active SLAM.

AI.tldr: RoboAtlas is a contextual Active SLAM framework that balances geometric exploration and semantic reasoning using a 3D semantic mapping system (OpenRoboVox). It achieves 100% task success in real-world tests and state-of-the-art 90.6% SR on GOAT-Bench, demonstrating that grounding foundation models with large-scale 3D semantic maps significantly improves performance.
AI.motivation: To address the need for an Active SLAM system that can adaptively balance geometric exploration and semantic reasoning in large-scale environments, moving beyond purely geometric approaches.
AI.method: Integrates frontier exploration, global semantic-map reasoning, and egocentric VLM-based reasoning using a contextual multi-armed bandit that transitions from exploration to semantically guided navigation as scene understanding improves.
AI.result: Achieves 100% task success on Unitree Go2 in real-world environments (1800 m², ~30k semantic instances). On GOAT-Bench "Val Unseen": 90.6% SR with GPT-4o (17.8 pp improvement), and 88.8% SR with Qwen2.5-VL-7B, outperforming all baselines using GPT-4o.
AI.conclusion: Grounding foundation models with large-scale 3D semantic maps enables robust and efficient contextual Active SLAM, and the semantic mapping framework itself contributes more to performance than simply replacing the underlying VLM.

title: Learning Robot Visual Navigation in Crowds via Intention-Aware Scene Representations
summary: Robot crowd navigation requires the ability to infer human intentions while accounting for the structural constraints of the environment. Currently, deep reinforcement learning (DRL) provides a promising method for learning navigation policies that understand human intentions. However, most of them rely on limited scene representations, treating pedestrians as simple 2D points and ignoring rich visual cues from both humans and the environment. To address this issue, we introduce iCrowdNav, a novel visual crowd navigation method with intention-aware scene representations, to encode behavioral and structural context from egocentric visual observations. Our method employs two key components: a spatio-temporal encoder for extracting occupancy features of the scene, and Intent-Interact Former (I$^2$ Former), an attention-based module that encodes human poses to infer pedestrians' motion intentions. These features are integrated into a compact state embedding that supports effective DRL policy training. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves superior performance over baselines, and real-world deployment demonstrates vision-based crowd navigation.

AI.tldr: iCrowdNav uses visual attention and pose-based intention inference for robot crowd navigation, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing DRL navigation methods treat pedestrians as simple 2D points, ignoring rich visual cues from humans and environments.
AI.method: Two components: (1) spatio-temporal encoder for occupancy features, (2) Intent-Interact Former (I² Former) for encoding human poses to infer motion intentions. Integrated into DRL policy training.
AI.result: Superior performance over baselines in extensive experiments; successful real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: Visual scene representations and intention-aware encoding improve crowd navigation in DRL policies.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning-Enhanced Event-Triggered Data-Driven Predictive Control for a 3D Cable-Driven Soft Robotic Arm
summary: Soft robots are challenging to control due to their nonlinear and time-varying dynamics. Data-enabled predictive control (DeePC) offers a model-free alternative by directly leveraging measured input-output trajectories to construct a predictive controller. However, its receding-horizon formulation requires solving a constrained optimization problem at every sampling instant, which can be computationally demanding for real-time deployment on resource-limited robotic platforms.To address this limitation, we propose an adaptive reinforcement-learning-based event-triggered DeePC (RL-ET-DeePC) framework for soft robotic control. A model-free RL policy is trained to determine when to invoke the DeePC optimizer based on the current system state representation, thereby reducing unnecessary optimization calls while preserving closed-loop performance.Simulation results show that RL-ET-DeePC reduces optimization frequency by up to 66% compared to periodic DeePC, while maintaining comparable tracking accuracy. Hardware experiments on a three-dimensional cable-driven soft robotic arm demonstrate zero-shot transfer, achieving a 34% reduction in optimization frequency with tracking accuracy comparable to periodic DeePC and more consistent performance than a static threshold-based event-triggered baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RL-ET-DeePC, a reinforcement-learning-based event-triggered data-enabled predictive control framework that reduces optimization frequency by up to 66% in simulation and 34% in hardware while maintaining tracking accuracy for soft robotic control.
AI.motivation: Soft robots are hard to control due to nonlinear/time-varying dynamics, and standard DeePC is computationally expensive for real-time deployment on resource-limited platforms.
AI.method: An RL policy is trained to decide when to trigger the DeePC optimizer based on system state, reducing unnecessary optimization calls.
AI.result: 66% reduction in optimization frequency in simulation; 34% reduction on a real 3D cable-driven soft robotic arm with comparable tracking accuracy.
AI.conclusion: RL-ET-DeePC effectively reduces computational burden while maintaining performance, and transfers zero-shot to real hardware.

title: ForceBand: Learning Forceful Manipulation with sEMG
summary: Human demonstrations are a scalable data source for learning robot manipulation policies. However, common sources of human demonstration data, such as motion-capture trajectories and internet videos, capture mostly motion and appearance while missing the contact forces that are critical for force-sensitive manipulation. In this paper, we introduce ForceBand, a low-cost wrist-worn sEMG system that turns human muscle activity into force-enriched demonstrations. We first collect a 10-hour multimodal dataset containing egocentric video, sEMG, IMU, and fingertip force measurements across diverse actions and objects. Using this dataset, we pre-train an EMG2Force model that predicts per-finger forces from sEMG and IMU signals. After a short user-specific calibration, users can collect target-task demonstrations using only ForceBand and video; EMG2Force then labels these demonstrations with per-finger force traces, producing force-augmented demonstrations for robot policy learning. Experiments show that ForceBand recovers fine-grained fingertip interactions with over 50% lower force prediction error than vision-based baselines and achieves an 87% success rate on pick, squeeze, and place tasks that require object-specific force control across objects with diverse shapes, sizes, and weights. Project website: https://forceband-emg.github.io

AI.tldr: ForceBand is a low-cost wrist-worn sEMG system that predicts per-finger forces from muscle activity, enabling force-enriched robot demonstrations from human video data.
AI.motivation: Human demonstrations used for robot learning lack contact force information crucial for force-sensitive manipulation tasks.
AI.method: Collected 10-hour multimodal dataset (video, sEMG, IMU, fingertip forces) → pre-trained EMG2Force model predicting per-finger forces → user-specific calibration → force-augmented demonstrations for robot policy learning.
AI.result: Outperforms vision-based baselines by >50% lower force prediction error; achieves 87% success rate on force-sensitive pick/squeeze/place tasks across diverse objects.
AI.conclusion: ForceBand effectively bridges the force gap in human demonstrations for robot learning using low-cost wearable sEMG sensing.

title: Learning Action Priors for Cross-embodiment Robot Manipulation
summary: Most Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models build on a Vision-Language Model (VLM) backbone by attaching an action module and optimizing the full policy jointly. This design inherits strong visual and linguistic priors from the VLM, but leaves the action module to learn physical motion almost from scratch.
